And if you want to sit in the front, you can also sit in the front. It is it is safe. I will not pop
quiz you. So wait 30 more seconds. So if you want to get a cup of coffee or something, you can come
through. My name is Amber Case. I go by Case. I'm an early blue sky adopter. And I am trained as a
cyborg anthropologist. So I look at how technology affects culture and how culture affects technology.
And today I want to get slightly metacognitive and think about how do we think about the future as
humans? And what does that actually mean? What is the future anyway? So I like the idea of local
minima. And who knows about local minima in here? You can raise your hand. Yay. Great. Good audience.
So my non-perfect explanation of local minima is you are trying to find either the highest or the
lowest point on a graph. But sometimes you can get stuck in this local minima. I don't know if this
thing works. But anyway, so you can see that like little shallow divot over there. And you can get
stuck in the shallow divot. And because all the other mountains around, you can say, well, I'm at the
lowest point. This is great. And the only way to get out of that is to walk around. And you can see
the meme about just walk around. It's fine. But most people might get stuck. And they might say,
well, I don't even know what it is to walk around. And so in this talk, I want to give you some ideas
on how to get out of the local minima of what we think about the future and what we think the future
is and learn how to walk around or different methods of walking around so that we can reach
a different point. Usually this is someone who says, you know, VR and AR are the future. Where did
they get that idea? That's usually a narrative. And just walking around can be the difference between
trying to develop something for another $80 billion again or, yeah. So one of my favorite people,
I call him the local big bird of Portland, Oregon, Ward Cunningham. He's one of my favorite friends,
but he created the Wiki. But also he has Cunningham's Law. The best way to get the right answer on the
internet is not to ask a question, but it's to post the wrong answer. But I also love this quote from
James Baldwin, which is, the purpose of art is to lay bare the questions that have been hidden by the
answers. And I think we have so many things that look like answers out there that it's incredibly hard
to understand what the questions are. And for most of technology, we can look at once something gets
adopted, it becomes normal, it becomes invisible. We don't question or talk about it. It just is.
We do not have a massive set of headlines that say, every person at Stanford wants to raise venture
capital in order to make the new light switch. It's a light switch. We don't even think about it because
it's just normal. So, so many things in our everyday lives are so normal because technically they're so
well-designed that we don't have to think about them anymore. And so my question to you, if you can do
anything after this talk, is to look at the things in your environment that are so well-designed, you no longer
notice them, and question, where did they come from? Who invented them? Why do they exist? And then ask
different questions, try to come up with, with the new questions. So, there's a lot of false narratives in
the way of thinking about what the next thing actually is. And really, innovation comes from the side. You
might be driving in a car, looking into the front, saying, "Oh, what's next? It's this future. It's this future." Or
looking behind at what the notion of the future was told to you was supposed to be the future. And you might not see it coming.
So, I started to interview people who had been sideswiped by the future. I was interviewing people that worked at
Blockbuster. And they said, "Yay, Blockbuster. It was great." And then somebody said, "Well, we're going to do this new
thing. You know, there's, there's, there's, of course, Redbox, and then there's Netflix." And they said that the majority of
people said, "Well, we're going to work on this thing." And there were a few people that said, "We're going to digitize content, and load it into the sky, and then download it
through people's machines." And it wasn't until people could clearly see that future, that they
could adopt it, and there was a pathway for them to get to it. And there were just a few people on the
edge that said, "Yeah, I can see this future. It makes sense. This is normal." And then, and then the future
sideswiped a bunch of people. I was trained at the Institute for the Future, which is a, at least 50 years of predicting the future. First, you
do not predict one future, you predict multiple futures. Second, you use the past to understand the
future. And three, you look at cosmotechnics, which is the idea that every culture has a technology, not
just Silicon Valley, and that it is 10,000 to a million years old, and it's site-specific based on
the geography, based on culture, based on history, based on food, and that we shouldn't narrow it into
technology and the future as the last 20 years, but that it is all time, and it's extremely sophisticated.
From wind catcher towers in Persia 2,000 years ago that allowed people to have modern air conditioning
and ice cream, we can think of everything not as this time right now, but all time. And this is the two
curves model. So the first curve is like a signal that masks the future. It's so hard to see the future
because the signal is so weak at first. And then the second curve comes up, and it is only when that first
curve declines that that second curve signal gets strong enough to be seen. My dad was an audio
engineer, so everything I think about is in terms of signal processing. I'm sorry. Then I started to
look at different different types of innovation. So Art Frye, who's a banjo player that worked at 3M,
was trying to make the strongest adhesive in history, and he failed tremendously. He made this kind of weak,
terrible adhesive that didn't work. And I think his wife at one point was like, "Hey,
I just put these things on the choir notes and brought them to church, and everybody loves them
because you can remove them and place them back on." And the sticky note was boring, the post-it note.
There's a different side that comes more from constraint. There are a lot of books and interesting
history on this, America's first paramedics, because people didn't have access. And it's a really
upsetting, tragic story. And then we got this incredible service that people use all the time
now, even though it's been really raised in price in the United States. But there's this idea that when
there is constraint and discrimination and non-access, really interesting things can come about based
on the community, based from the ground up. It's not a top-down innovation space. It's not a company
saying, "The future is the metaverse," and then not understanding anything.
I got to have dinner with this person. I spoke at a conference with him, and I saw him eating alone,
and I was like, "Can I eat with you?" And he created - he's a really curious person, and his mother was
one of the hidden figures that worked at NASA. But he tried to figure out how he could get an education

the microphone that I'm using is using his technology. He's a really, really curious person.
But he followed it. And so a lot of really interesting inventions came forward, not because someone was
trying to make something, but because they were interested in a very small thing and asked an
interesting question. Also, if anyone knows why, Mario is very small on the screen. It was because of
a constraint. So instead of pulling the camera away, which would have required more memory and processing
power, to render a larger portion of the screen, the inventor suggested shrinking the size of the
character sprite. And so the whole game could fit into 32K and about 40K total with the full cartridge
data with the code and graphics. Steve Wozniak, the co-founder of Apple, was up all night,
every night, every night, because he really wanted a personal computer. And so he's constantly thinking,
how could you fit the chips onto the motherboard in order to do this? And he's working at HP at the
time. And of course, the famous thing was, he said, "Hey, we invented this small computer." And his boss
said, "No one will want a small desktop computer, and there's no way it could be that small." And so
Apple was born in the garage. Also, laziness. The first webcam was for coffee. It was in Cambridge,
England. The idea was, "We don't want to go to the coffee all the way and see that it was empty,
so we're going to make a webcam." And this was the first kind of wormhole to see another space when
you were somewhere else. And then, of course, it got put on the web in 1993 and became an artifact of
the web. So why are we as humans, not all humans, but many, many humans, especially in the West and
especially in Silicon Valley, very obsessed with the future? What is this idea of the future?
And I use this ostrich thing. One part of it was standing on two feet. When we started to stand on
two feet, we had this huge foundational shift in evolution that was preceding large brain development,
and really acting as a primary driver for neurological and cognitive advancement. Because
you suddenly had your hands free, everything became about using tools and working with systems over

down when we think about things. And it's a very different way of thinking than standing up or sitting
in an abstracted couch that's extremely, you know, it's a different way of thinking.
This is the Chick-Saw tribe. Of course, here's an elder telling a story to a bunch of kids. But I was told by
by one of the co-creators of Calm Technology that he was going to explore different ways of being,
and how information got passed down through generations. And he was with a First Nations
tribe, and he noticed that all of the kids were not ready to listen. And so the elder said,
"Go swim across that river and come back." So all the kids get into the river, they swim across the shore,
and they come back, and they're extremely exhausted. And then they're sitting down,
and they're ready to listen to a story. But the most important thing was this is not a PhD level.
This is a PhD level of information getting given in a narrative story that doesn't require a PhD or
a research paper to be published. It's, you know, how do you tell the pH of the soil? Well, we've placed
these indicator plants around so you can see when this is ready to plant. And now all you have to do is
go and observe and you get this information. But you tell it in an interesting story because our brains
are really, really excited about narrative information. Extremely excited. So excited that,
as the first keynote was talking about, if it's in a nice narrative and you read about it with enough
density that it looks like a thing, that we think that we have the knowledge unless we're asked to
redo that or, you know, fix a toilet. But the important part about it is that when it's given in this
structure multi-generationally, you get all sorts of interesting information.
And sometimes narratives can really go crazy. This is the railway mania from the 1840s to 1860s.
In my opinion, this is still recent history. I don't think of history as like 500 years ago starts to
get like, you know, that's like what we would think of as like the 80s to me. And then, you know,
1000 years ago, 2000 years ago, that's like, that's starting to get that's like kind of contemporary in
a sense, like, you know, that'd be like the 50s. Because our choice of how we experience time and
understand what time is, is just a filter and a lens that we have. If we take that aperture and change it,
we can start to understand how much rhyme there is out there. So this railway mania was hilarious and
terrible. It was basically like an AI bubble. I think one fifth of people at that point in time,
I think at least in England had invested and had shares in the railway companies.
That was everyone going, you need to buy this, it's going to go up like crazy. It's like the new crypto
and we're going to pave the way literally to a future in which we're going to have all these
amazing things. The narrative was so exciting that everyone went crazy about it. And then it crashed
terribly. But all of that money that went in through, you know, the general public holding the bag,
ended up paving, actually making rail possible. And so it was a really interesting thing. We can see
parallels with the dot com boom, we can see parallels today, that oftentimes the public will get hoodwinked
into a narrative of the future that's so exciting and spicy that they will fund it, it will crash,
and then on the dying flames, the phoenix will rise of the real thing. So if we ask this question again,

here today is where did the term artificial intelligence come from? We like to talk about
this a lot. Does anyone know? Yay, great, one person. This is, it's very rare that even one person.
When we use a term a lot, it doesn't mean that it's the real term, and it also doesn't mean that we know

of this, but I'm going to give an abridged version. This is Norbert Wiener. He's considered the father of
cybernetics. The idea of cybernetics is the thing that really predates AI. It's from Kubernetes, meaning
Helms person. It's from Greek. Here's a person getting a signal and making a decision. So it's a human and a

automation, but those automations are producing signals that can be human readable, and that the

Here's a sensor with a threshold. There's a difference. The controller sees it. There's an action that,
you know, feeds back into the system. So over time, you can drive a car which could kill anyone for no good

can do for you well as an automation, understanding what to steer. So some of it is making the invisible
visible. This thing is an automation. Here's the information. Here's how to make a decision.
Now, the problem is that Norbert Wiener apparently was a very talkative person. He would go into every
room and say, well, that's cybernetics. It was true. And he wouldn't stop talking. He just wouldn't

one reason for inventing the term AI was to escape association with cybernetics. I wish to avoid

You don't have a system where all things happen. You have a system where a gradient amount of things
happen. And then that gradient shifts over time and goes into dynamic equilibrium because that's how

I didn't have access to television except for Star Trek. And I love these book series because the one

Because history isn't that long ago and the 1900s, 1800s were not that long ago, we still have this memory.
Most people have gone through something terrible in their history, not that long ago. And there's this

And when they became cheap enough, there was a Nobel prize awarded for it. And we thought that the

So I would love to encourage you to beware of times when telling stories about doing the real thing
are more rewarded than actually doing the real thing. And I think this is a particular era in which

You don't even get to watch war games as your first thing. So would you drive over this bridge in a

But really, I'm very interested in cognitive security. Where do you get your thoughts? Where did

One final thought. The question is, what do most people have in common? Engineers, doctors,

And the main thing is that most people in the past would have an apprenticeship.
There would be a history, or especially in art, before somebody studies art history and joins

So if we have humans alongside nature, we should also have humans alongside technology.
